question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.take (buy offer, seller=victim)] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized call Midnight.take with taker=victim as seller against a buy offer at tick=0 (price near 1e18), where sellerAssets ≈ units and buyerAssets ≈ units + settlementFee, causing victim to receive sellerAssets of loanToken while creating units of debt — and if victim has no collateral, the health check at the end of take (isHealthy) will fail with SellerIsLiquidatable, reverting the transaction — but can the attacker craft a scenario where victim has just enough collateral to pass health check while still creating bad debt that reduces lender credits below loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight)? Preconditions: victim has minimal collateral; attacker is authorized; a buy offer exists at low tick. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.take(buyOffer, ..., taker=victim, ...); assert isHealthy(market, id, victim) barely passes; assert victim.debt > 0; warp time to reduce oracle price; assert victim becomes liquidatable; liquidate to realize bad debt. Invariant tested: take must not create positions that immediately generate bad debt. Scoped impact: insolvency via authorized take creating undercollateralized debt. Proof idea: fuzz oracle price drop after take and assert bad debt realization reduces lender credits.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized calls IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).setIsAuthorized(authorization.authorized, authorization.isAuthorized, authorization.authorizer) — where the Midnight.setIsAuthorized requires require(onBehalf == msg.sender || isAuthorized[onBehalf][msg.sender]) — meaning EcrecoverAuthorizer (msg.sender) must be authorized by authorization.authorizer (onBehalf) on Midnight, and if EcrecoverAuthorizer is NOT authorized by the authorizer, the call reverts — but can an attacker create a market where EcrecoverAuthorizer is the enterGate or liquidatorGate, causing canIncreaseCredit/canLiquidate to return true for EcrecoverAuthorizer, and then use this to bypass gate checks? Preconditions: attacker creates a market with enterGate=EcrecoverAuthorizer; EcrecoverAuthorizer does not implement IEnterGate. Call sequence: attacker calls Midnight.touchMarket(market{enterGate=EcrecoverAuthorizer}); attacker calls Midnight.take with buyer whose credit increases; assert canIncreaseCredit call to EcrecoverAuthorizer reverts or returns wrong value. Invariant tested: gate contracts must implement correct interfaces; EcrecoverAuthorizer must not be usable as a gate. Scoped impact: insolvency via gate bypass enabling unauthorized credit increase. Proof idea: assert Midnight.take reverts when enterGate=EcrecoverAuthorizer and credit increases.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.withdraw] Can an attacker perform a griefing-to-insolvency attack by: (1) obtaining authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized for multiple victims, (2) calling Midnight.withdraw(market, 1, victim_i, attacker) for each victim to drain 1 unit from each, (3) causing rounding in pendingFeeDecrease = position.pendingFee.mulDivUp(1, position.credit) to round up to 1 even when pendingFee/credit < 1, and (4) accumulating rounding errors across many victims such that the sum of pendingFee decreases exceeds the actual fee owed, causing continuousFeeCredit to be underfunded relative to loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight)? Preconditions: many victims have credit with small pendingFee; attacker is authorized for each; market has non-zero continuousFee. Call sequence: for each victim: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.withdraw(market, 1, victim, attacker); assert sum of pendingFeeDecrease > actual fee accrued. Invariant tested: rounding in pendingFee accounting must not cause continuousFeeCredit to exceed loanToken balance. Scoped impact: insolvency via accumulated rounding errors in fee accounting. Proof idea: fuzz N victims with small credit/pendingFee ratios and assert continuousFeeCredit <= loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight).

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.take] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized call Midnight.take on behalf of victim as buyer (taker=victim, offer.buy=false) at a tick where buyerAssets > sellerAssets (due to settlement fee), causing claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] += buyerAssets - sellerAssets, and if the payer (buyerCallback or msg.sender) is the attacker who provides insufficient tokens (fee-on-transfer token), the actual loanToken received by Midnight is less than buyerAssets - sellerAssets, causing claimableSettlementFee to exceed actual loanToken balance? Preconditions: loanToken is a fee-on-transfer token; victim has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer; attacker is authorized; a sell offer exists. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.take(sellOffer, ..., taker=victim, ...); assert claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] > loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight) - (withdrawable + continuousFeeCredit). Invariant tested: claimableSettlementFee must not exceed actual loanToken balance minus other obligations. Scoped impact: insolvency — settlement fee claim exceeds actual token balance. Proof idea: deploy fee-on-transfer loanToken and assert balance invariant after take.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.liquidate] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized call Midnight.liquidate(market, collateralIndex, seizedAssets, 0, victim, true, attacker, address(0), '') in post-maturity mode with seizedAssets = victim.collateral[collateralIndex] (full seizure), receiving all collateral while repaying 0 debt units (since repaidUnits = seizedAssets.mulDivUp(liquidatedCollatPrice, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, lif) rounds up to 0 when price is very small), leaving victim's debt unchanged but collateral drained, causing collateralToken.balanceOf(Midnight) to be insufficient for other borrowers? Preconditions: market is post-maturity; victim has debt and collateral; oracle price is very small (near 0 but not 0); attacker is authorized. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; warp to post-maturity; attacker calls Midnight.liquidate(market, index, victim.collateral[index], 0, victim, true, attacker, address(0), ''); assert repaidUnits == 0 due to rounding; assert collateralToken.balanceOf(Midnight) decreased by seizedAssets. Invariant tested: liquidation must not allow full collateral seizure with zero debt repayment. Scoped impact: insolvency — collateral balance drained without corresponding debt reduction. Proof idea: fuzz oracle price near 0 and assert repaidUnits > 0 for any seizedAssets > 0.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.withdraw] Can an attacker exploit the interaction between bad debt realization and authorization by: (1) obtaining authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, (2) triggering bad debt realization via Midnight.liquidate(market, 0, 0, 0, victim, false, ...) to increase lossFactor, (3) calling Midnight.updatePosition(market, lender) to slash lender's credit, and (4) calling Midnight.withdraw(market, lender.credit_before_slash, lender, attacker) — but since credit was slashed, the withdraw reverts with underflow — or alternatively, can the attacker withdraw the pre-slash credit amount if updatePosition is not called first, draining more than the post-slash credit? Preconditions: victim has bad debt; lender has credit; attacker is authorized for lender; lossFactor > 0. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized for lender; attacker calls Midnight.liquidate(0,0,0) to realize bad debt; attacker calls Midnight.withdraw(market, lender.credit_stale, lender, attacker) WITHOUT calling updatePosition first; assert withdraw uses stale credit value. Invariant tested: withdraw must apply loss factor before allowing withdrawal. Scoped impact: insolvency — lender withdraws more than post-slash credit entitlement. Proof idea: assert Midnight.withdraw calls _updatePosition before processing withdrawal amount.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.take (reduceOnly offer)] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized call Midnight.take on behalf of victim as seller against a reduceOnly=true buy offer, where the offer's reduceOnly constraint requires sellerDebtIncrease == 0 (seller's debt must not increase), but the attacker crafts the take such that victim's credit is exactly consumed and then debt increases by 1 unit due to rounding in sellerCreditDecrease = min(units, sellerPos.credit), causing the reduceOnly check to fail and revert — or alternatively, can the attacker use reduceOnly to force victim into a state where credit is drained but debt remains, creating an undercollateralized position? Preconditions: victim has credit and debt; a reduceOnly buy offer exists; attacker is authorized. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.take(reduceOnlyOffer, ..., taker=victim, units=victim.credit+1); assert MakerCreditOrDebtIncreased revert; then try units=victim.credit exactly; assert victim.credit==0 and victim.debt unchanged. Invariant tested: reduceOnly must not be usable to drain credit while leaving debt. Scoped impact: insolvency via credit drain leaving undercollateralized debt. Proof idea: fuzz units around victim.credit and assert health invariant after take.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.repay (callback)] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized call Midnight.repay(market, units, victim, maliciousCallback, data) where maliciousCallback.onRepay reenters Midnight.withdraw(market, units, victim, attacker) — since repay increases marketState.withdrawable BEFORE the callback and BEFORE pulling tokens, the reentrant withdraw can drain the just-increased withdrawable, and then the repay's safeTransferFrom pulls tokens from the callback (which may have already spent them), causing loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight) to be less than marketState.withdrawable? Preconditions: victim has debt; attacker is authorized; maliciousCallback implements IRepayCallback and reenters withdraw. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.repay(market, units, victim, maliciousCallback, ''); inside onRepay: call Midnight.withdraw(market, units, victim, attacker); assert loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight) < marketState.withdrawable after repay completes. Invariant tested: repay callback must not enable reentrancy that drains withdrawable before token transfer. Scoped impact: insolvency — withdrawable exceeds actual loanToken balance. Proof idea: implement IRepayCallback that reenters withdraw and assert balance invariant.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker who has been authorized by victim on Midnight (isAuthorized[victim][attacker]=true) call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N} signed by attacker (not victim), passing the signer check since isAuthorized[victim][attacker]=true, to consume victim's nonce N and prevent victim from using nonce N for a legitimate authorization — effectively allowing any authorized address to grief the authorizer's nonce sequence and block future EcrecoverAuthorizer-based authorizations? Preconditions: isAuthorized[victim][attacker]=true on Midnight; victim has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer on Midnight; victim has pending signed authorizations with nonce=N. Call sequence: attacker signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=false, nonce=N}; attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; assert nonce[victim] == N+1; victim's pending authorization with nonce=N now reverts with InvalidNonce. Invariant tested: nonce consumption must require the authorizer's own signature, not any authorized delegate's signature. Scoped impact: insolvency via nonce griefing blocking legitimate authorization revocations. Proof idea: assert EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized succeeds when signer is authorized delegate and assert nonce incremented.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.withdrawCollateral] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized call Midnight.withdrawCollateral(market, collateralIndex, assets, victim, attacker) where victim has debt, and the health check isHealthy(market, id, victim) passes because the oracle price has been manipulated to a high value at the time of withdrawal — but the oracle price then drops, making victim unhealthy — and since the attacker has already withdrawn collateral, victim's position is now undercollateralized with insufficient collateral to cover debt, generating bad debt that reduces all lenders' credit below loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight)? Preconditions: victim has debt and collateral; oracle price is temporarily high; attacker is authorized. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; oracle returns high price; attacker calls Midnight.withdrawCollateral(market, index, maxAssets, victim, attacker); oracle price drops; victim becomes unhealthy; liquidate to realize bad debt. Invariant tested: collateral withdrawal must not enable subsequent bad debt creation. Scoped impact: insolvency via oracle-assisted collateral drain creating bad debt. Proof idea: use mock oracle with price manipulation and assert bad debt after withdrawal.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.take (group consumption)] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized call Midnight.take on behalf of victim-maker (as taker=victim, offer.buy=true, offer.maker=victim) — wait, SelfTake() prevents maker==taker — so instead, can the attacker call Midnight.setConsumed(group, 0, victim) to reset consumed[victim][group] to 0 (which reverts with AlreadyConsumed if consumed > 0), or call setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim) to cancel all offers, and then take victim's offers at stale prices before victim can update them, draining loanToken from Midnight? Preconditions: victim-maker has active offers with consumed < maxAssets; attacker is authorized; market has loanToken. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim); assert all victim's offers in group are cancelled; attacker takes remaining offers at favorable prices. Invariant tested: offer cancellation via setConsumed must not enable front-running that drains protocol assets. Scoped impact: insolvency via offer manipulation draining loanToken balance. Proof idea: assert setConsumed(type(uint256).max) cancels all offers and no further takes succeed.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.claimContinuousFee] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized manipulate the continuousFeeCredit accounting by: (1) calling Midnight.updatePosition(market, victim) repeatedly to crystallize fees into continuousFeeCredit, (2) then calling Midnight.withdraw(market, victim.credit, victim, attacker) to drain victim's credit, and (3) since claimContinuousFee decreases both continuousFeeCredit and withdrawable, verify whether the sequence of updatePosition + withdraw can cause continuousFeeCredit > marketState.withdrawable, making the fee claim impossible and the fee permanently locked in the contract? Preconditions: victim has credit with large pendingFee; market has non-zero continuousFee; attacker is authorized. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.updatePosition(market, victim) multiple times; attacker calls Midnight.withdraw(market, victim.credit, victim, attacker); assert continuousFeeCredit > marketState.withdrawable. Invariant tested: continuousFeeCredit must always be claimable from withdrawable. Scoped impact: insolvency — fee credit exceeds withdrawable, making fees unclaimable. Proof idea: fuzz time elapsed and assert continuousFeeCredit <= marketState.withdrawable at all times.

Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; oracle returns high price; attacker calls Midnight.withdrawCollateral(market, index, maxAssets, victim, attacker); oracle price drops; victim becomes unhealthy; liquidate to realize bad debt. Invariant tested: collateral withdrawal must not enable subsequent bad debt creation. Scoped impact: insolvency via oracle-assisted collateral drain creating bad debt. Proof idea: use mock oracle with price manipulation and assert bad debt after withdrawal.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.take (group consumption)] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized call Midnight.take on behalf of victim-maker (as taker=victim, offer.buy=true, offer.maker=victim) — wait, SelfTake() prevents maker==taker — so instead, can the attacker call Midnight.setConsumed(group, 0, victim) to reset consumed[victim][group] to 0 (which reverts with AlreadyConsumed if consumed > 0), or call setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim) to cancel all offers, and then take victim's offers at stale prices before victim can update them, draining loanToken from Midnight? Preconditions: victim-maker has active offers with consumed < maxAssets; attacker is authorized; market has loanToken. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, victim); assert all victim's offers in group are cancelled; attacker takes remaining offers at favorable prices. Invariant tested: offer cancellation via setConsumed must not enable front-running that drains protocol assets. Scoped impact: insolvency via offer manipulation draining loanToken balance. Proof idea: assert setConsumed(type(uint256).max) cancels all offers and no further takes succeed.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.claimContinuousFee] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized manipulate the continuousFeeCredit accounting by: (1) calling Midnight.updatePosition(market, victim) repeatedly to crystallize fees into continuousFeeCredit, (2) then calling Midnight.withdraw(market, victim.credit, victim, attacker) to drain victim's credit, and (3) since claimContinuousFee decreases both continuousFeeCredit and withdrawable, verify whether the sequence of updatePosition + withdraw can cause continuousFeeCredit > marketState.withdrawable, making the fee claim impossible and the fee permanently locked in the contract? Preconditions: victim has credit with large pendingFee; market has non-zero continuousFee; attacker is authorized. Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.updatePosition(market, victim) multiple times; attacker calls Midnight.withdraw(market, victim.credit, victim, attacker); assert continuousFeeCredit > marketState.withdrawable. Invariant tested: continuousFeeCredit must always be claimable from withdrawable. Scoped impact: insolvency — fee credit exceeds withdrawable, making fees unclaimable. Proof idea: fuzz time elapsed and assert continuousFeeCredit <= marketState.withdrawable at all times.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.take (buy offer, seller=victim)] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized call Midnight.take with taker=victim as seller against a buy offer at tick=0 (price near 1e18), where sellerAssets ≈ units and buyerAssets ≈ units + settlementFee, causing victim to receive sellerAssets of loanToken while creating units of debt — and if victim has no collateral, the health check at the end of take (isHealthy) will fail with SellerIsLiquidatable, reverting the transaction — but can the attacker craft a scenario where victim has just enough collateral to pass health check while still creating bad debt that reduces lender credits below loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight)? [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an attacker exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized calls IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).setIsAuthorized(authorization.authorized, authorization.isAuthorized, authorization.authorizer) — where the Midnight.setIsAuthorized requires require(onBehalf == msg.sender || isAuthorized[onBehalf][msg.sender]) — meaning EcrecoverAuthorizer (msg.sender) must be authorized by authorization.authorizer (onBehalf) on Midnight, and if EcrecoverAuthorizer is NOT authorized by the authorizer, the call reverts — but can an attacker create a market where EcrecoverAuthorizer is the enterGate or liquidatorGate, causing canIncreaseCredit/canLiquidate to return true for EcrecoverAuthorizer, and then use this to bypass gate checks? Preconditions: attacker creates a market with enterGate=EcrecoverAuthorizer; EcrecoverAuthorizer does not implement IEnterGate. Call sequence: attacker calls Midnight.touchMarket(market{enterGate=EcrecoverAuthorizer}); attacker calls Midnight.take with buyer whose credit increases; assert canIncreaseCredit call to EcrecoverAuthorizer reverts or returns wrong value. Invariant tested: gate contracts must implement correct interfaces; EcrecoverAuthorizer must not be usable as a gate. Scoped impact: insolvency via gate bypass enabling unauthorized credit increase. Proof idea: assert Midnight.take reverts when enterGate=EcrecoverAuthorizer and credit increases.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.withdraw] Can an attacker perform a griefing-to-insolvency attack by: (1) obtaining authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized for multiple victims, (2) calling Midnight.withdraw(market, 1, victim_i, attacker) for each victim to drain 1 unit from each, (3) causing rounding in pendingFeeDecrease = position.pendingFee.mulDivUp(1, position.credit) to round up to 1 even when pendingFee/credit < 1, and (4) accumulating rounding errors across many victims such that the sum of pendingFee decreases exceeds the actual fee owed, causing continuousFeeCredit to be underfunded relative to loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight)? Invariant tested: rounding in pendingFee accounting must not cause continuousFeeCredit to exceed loanToken balance. Scoped impact: insolvency via accumulated rounding errors in fee accounting. Proof idea: fuzz N victims with small credit/pendingFee ratios and assert continuousFeeCredit <= loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight).

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.take] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized call Midnight.take on behalf of victim as buyer (taker=victim, offer.buy=false) at a tick where buyerAssets > sellerAssets (due to settlement fee), causing claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] += buyerAssets - sellerAssets, and if the payer (buyerCallback or msg.sender) is the attacker who provides insufficient tokens (fee-on-transfer token), the actual loanToken received by Midnight is less than buyerAssets - sellerAssets, causing claimableSettlementFee to exceed actual loanToken balance? Proof idea: deploy fee-on-transfer loanToken and assert balance invariant after take.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.liquidate] Can an attacker authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized call Midnight.liquidate(market, collateralIndex, seizedAssets, 0, victim, true, attacker, address(0), '') in post-maturity mode with seizedAssets = victim.collateral[collateralIndex] (full seizure), receiving all collateral while repaying 0 debt units (since repaidUnits = seizedAssets.mulDivUp(liquidatedCollatPrice, ORACLE_PRICE_SCALE).mulDivUp(WAD, lif) rounds up to 0 when price is very small), leaving victim's debt unchanged but collateral drained, causing collateralToken.balanceOf(Midnight) to be insufficient for other borrowers? [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Critical Protocol insolvency where Midnight contract token balances cannot cover collateral, withdrawable assets, or claimable fees'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / Midnight.withdraw] Can an attacker exploit the interaction between bad debt realization and authorization by: (1) obtaining authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized, (2) triggering bad debt realization via Midnight.liquidate(market, 0, 0, 0, victim, false, ...) to increase lossFactor, (3) calling Midnight.updatePosition(market, lender) to slash lender's credit, and (4) calling Midnight.withdraw(market, lender.credit_before_slash, lender, attacker) — but since credit was slashed, the withdraw reverts with underflow — or alternatively, can the attacker withdraw the pre-slash credit amount if updatePosition is not called first, draining more than the post-slash credit? Call sequence: attacker calls EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized; attacker calls Midnight.repay(market, units, victim, maliciousCallback, ''); inside onRepay: call Midnight.withdraw(market, units, victim, attacker); assert loanToken.balanceOf(Midnight) < marketState.withdrawable after repay completes. Proof idea: implement IRepayCallback that reenters withdraw and assert balance invariant.

Invariant tested: nonce consumption must require the authorizer's own signature, not any authorized delegate's signature. Scoped impact: insolvency via nonce griefing blocking legitimate authorization revocations. Proof idea: assert EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized succeeds when signer is authorized delegate and assert nonce incremented.
